name: Jain Poha
description: Light and fluffy flattened rice tempered with mustard seeds, curry leaves, and asafoetida — a classic breakfast without onion or potato
steps: Wash poha in water, drain and let it sit for 5 minutes until soft. Sprinkle turmeric and salt, mix gently.
Heat oil in a pan. Add mustard seeds and let them splutter. Add cumin seeds, curry leaves, and green chilies.
Add peanuts and roast until golden. Add asafoetida and stir.
Add the soaked poha and mix gently. Add sugar and adjust salt.
Cook on low heat for 3-4 minutes, stirring gently. Squeeze lemon juice and garnish with coriander.

name: Moong Dal Cheela
description: Crispy savory pancakes made from ground moong dal batter, spiced with cumin and green chilies
steps: Soak moong dal for 4 hours. Drain and grind to a smooth batter with green chilies and minimal water.
Add cumin seeds, asafoetida, salt, and chopped coriander to the batter. Mix well.
Heat a non-stick pan, grease lightly with oil. Pour a ladleful of batter and spread in a circle.
Cook on medium heat until edges turn golden. Flip and cook the other side.
Serve hot with green chutney or curd.

name: Rava Upma
description: South Indian semolina porridge tempered with mustard seeds, cashews, and curry leaves
steps: Dry roast semolina on medium heat until lightly golden and aromatic. Set aside.
Heat oil and ghee. Add mustard seeds, urad dal, chana dal, and cashews. Fry until golden.
Add curry leaves, green chilies, and asafoetida. Sauté for 30 seconds.
Add water and salt. Bring to a boil. Slowly add roasted semolina while stirring continuously to avoid lumps.
Cook on low heat for 3-4 minutes, stirring. Cover and let steam for 2 minutes. Add lemon juice and garnish with coriander.

name: Besan Cheela
description: Crispy gram flour pancakes with capsicum, tomatoes, and spices — a protein-rich breakfast in minutes
steps: Mix besan with water to a smooth, lump-free batter. It should be pourable but not too thin.
Add chopped capsicum, tomato, green chilies, cumin seeds, turmeric, red chili powder, asafoetida, and salt. Mix well.
Heat a non-stick pan and grease lightly with oil. Pour batter and spread in a thin circle.
Cook on medium heat until the bottom turns golden. Drizzle oil around edges, flip and cook the other side.
Serve hot with green chutney and curd.

name: Sabudana Khichdi
description: Tapioca pearls tossed with roasted peanuts, cumin, and green chilies — a fasting favorite that works any day
steps: Wash sabudana 2-3 times until water runs clear. Soak in just enough water to cover for 6 hours or overnight. Drain completely.
Heat ghee in a pan. Add cumin seeds, green chilies, and curry leaves. Let cumin splutter.
Add coarsely ground peanuts and roast for a minute.
Add soaked sabudana, salt, and sugar. Toss gently on medium heat for 5-7 minutes until translucent and non-sticky.
Squeeze lemon juice, garnish with coriander leaves. Serve warm.

name: Methi Thepla
description: Gujarati spiced flatbreads with fenugreek leaves, perfect for breakfast or travel snacks
steps: Mix wheat flour, fenugreek leaves, curd, oil, and all spices. Knead into a soft dough using water as needed. Rest for 15 minutes.
Divide dough into golf-ball-sized portions. Roll each into a thin circle.
Heat a tawa on medium heat. Place thepla on it, cook until small bubbles appear.
Flip and apply oil. Cook until golden spots appear on both sides.
Serve hot with curd, pickle, or chundo (sweet mango pickle).

name: Khaman Dhokla
description: Steamed gram flour cakes with a tangy-sweet tempering of mustard seeds, sesame, and green chilies
steps: Mix besan, curd, water, turmeric, sugar, and salt into a smooth batter. Let rest for 10 minutes.
Grease a dhokla plate. Add eno fruit salt to batter, mix gently. Pour into the plate immediately.
Steam in a steamer for 15 minutes until a toothpick comes out clean. Let cool slightly and cut into squares.
For tempering: heat oil, add mustard seeds, sesame seeds, green chilies, and curry leaves. Add 1/4 cup water, sugar, and lemon juice. Pour over dhokla.
Garnish with coriander and grated coconut. Serve with green chutney.

name: Jain Masala Dosa
description: Crispy fermented rice-lentil crepes filled with a spiced paneer and peas masala instead of the usual potato
steps: Soak rice and urad dal with fenugreek seeds separately for 6-8 hours. Grind to a smooth batter. Ferment overnight.
For filling: heat oil, add mustard seeds, curry leaves, green chilies, and asafoetida. Add tomatoes and cook until soft.
Add peas and turmeric. Cook 5 minutes. Add crumbled paneer, salt, and mix well. The filling should be dry.
Heat a dosa tawa. Pour a ladle of batter and spread in a circle. Drizzle oil. Cook until crispy and golden.
Place filling in the center, fold the dosa. Serve with coconut chutney and sambar.

name: Idli with Coconut Chutney
description: Soft steamed rice cakes served with fresh coconut chutney — a light and wholesome breakfast
steps: Soak rice and urad dal with fenugreek seeds separately for 6-8 hours. Grind to a smooth batter, ferment overnight.
Add salt to the fermented batter and mix gently. Grease idli molds, pour batter, and steam for 10-12 minutes.
For chutney: grind coconut, roasted chana dal, green chilies with a little water to a smooth paste.
For tempering: heat oil, add mustard seeds and curry leaves. Pour over chutney.
Serve idlis hot with coconut chutney.

name: Paneer Paratha
description: Stuffed whole wheat flatbreads with spiced crumbled paneer filling, cooked in ghee until golden
steps: Knead wheat flour with water and a pinch of salt into a soft dough. Rest for 15 minutes.
Mix grated paneer with green chilies, coriander, cumin powder, red chili powder, and salt.
Divide dough into balls. Roll each, place paneer filling in the center, seal and roll out gently into a circle.
Heat a tawa. Cook paratha on medium heat, applying ghee on both sides until golden and crispy.
Serve hot with curd and pickle.

name: Matar Samosa
description: Crispy deep-fried pastry pockets filled with spiced green peas and paneer — no potato needed
steps: Make dough: Mix flour, 4 tbsp oil, salt, and water. Knead into a stiff dough. Rest 20 minutes.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Add peas and cook for 5 minutes. Add spices and paneer. Mash peas slightly. Cool.
Divide dough into balls. Roll each into an oval, cut in half. Form a cone with each half, fill with peas mixture, and seal edges with water.
Heat oil to 160°C. Deep fry samosas on low-medium heat until golden and crispy, about 8-10 minutes.
Drain on paper towels. Serve with green chutney and tamarind chutney.

name: Khandvi
description: Delicate rolled gram flour sheets with a tempering of mustard seeds, sesame, and coconut — a Gujarati delicacy
steps: Mix besan, curd, water, turmeric, and salt into a smooth lump-free batter.
Cook batter on medium heat, stirring continuously for 10-12 minutes until it becomes thick and leaves the sides of the pan.
Immediately spread thin layers on the back of greased plates. Let cool for 5 minutes.
Cut into strips and roll tightly. Arrange on a plate.
For tempering: heat oil, add mustard seeds, sesame seeds, green chilies, and curry leaves. Pour over khandvi. Garnish with coconut and coriander.

name: Capsicum Pakora
description: Crispy battered capsicum rings fried until golden — a crunchy tea-time snack
steps: Mix besan, rice flour, red chili powder, turmeric, carom seeds, asafoetida, and salt. Add water to make a thick batter.
Cut capsicum into thick rings. Dip each ring in the batter.
Heat oil to 170°C. Fry battered rings in batches until golden and crispy.
Drain on paper towels. Serve hot with green chutney or ketchup.

name: Paneer Tikka
description: Marinated paneer cubes grilled with capsicum and tomatoes in a spiced yogurt coating
steps: Mix curd, besan, all spice powders, lemon juice, oil, and salt into a smooth marinade.
Add paneer cubes, capsicum, and tomato chunks. Coat well. Marinate for 1 hour in the fridge.
Thread onto skewers alternating paneer, capsicum, and tomato.
Grill on a hot tawa or oven at 220°C for 10-12 minutes, turning once, until charred spots appear. Brush with oil.
Sprinkle chaat masala and lemon juice. Serve hot with mint chutney.

name: Mathri
description: Flaky, crispy savory crackers seasoned with cumin and ajwain — a traditional tea-time treat
steps: Mix flour, semolina, cumin seeds, ajwain, pepper, and salt. Rub in ghee until mixture is crumbly.
Add water slowly and knead into a stiff dough. Rest for 15 minutes.
Roll out dough to medium thickness. Cut into diamond or circle shapes. Prick each with a fork.
Heat oil to 150°C (low-medium heat). Fry mathris slowly until golden and flaky, about 6-8 minutes per batch.
Drain and cool completely. Store in airtight container — stays crispy for weeks.

name: Dal Tadka
description: Creamy toor dal finished with a sizzling tempering of cumin, mustard seeds, dried red chilies, and asafoetida
steps: Wash toor dal, pressure cook with turmeric, salt, and 3 cups water for 3-4 whistles. Mash well.
Add chopped tomatoes and red chili powder to the dal. Simmer for 10 minutes until tomatoes are soft.
For tadka: heat ghee in a small pan. Add cumin seeds, mustard seeds, dried red chilies, curry leaves, and asafoetida.
When seeds splutter, pour the sizzling tadka over the dal. Mix well.
Garnish with fresh coriander. Serve with steamed rice or roti.

name: Jain Palak Paneer
description: Silky spinach gravy with soft paneer cubes — made rich with cashew paste instead of onion-garlic base
steps: Blanch spinach in boiling water for 2 minutes. Transfer to ice water. Drain and blend into a smooth puree with green chilies.
Soak cashews in warm water for 15 minutes. Grind to a smooth paste.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Add chopped tomato and cook until soft. Add cashew paste and cook for 2 minutes.
Add spinach puree, turmeric, salt, and garam masala. Simmer for 5 minutes. Add paneer cubes and cream.
Cook for 3 more minutes. Serve hot with naan or jeera rice.

name: Jain Paneer Butter Masala
description: Rich, creamy tomato-cashew gravy with soft paneer cubes — restaurant-style without onion or garlic
steps: Boil tomatoes until soft. Cool and blend to a smooth puree. Soak cashews and blend to a paste.
Heat butter, add asafoetida and cumin. Add tomato puree, cook on medium heat for 10 minutes until oil separates.
Add cashew paste, all spice powders, sugar, and salt. Cook for 5 minutes, stirring continuously.
Add 1/2 cup water to adjust consistency. Add paneer cubes and simmer for 5 minutes.
Add cream and kasuri methi (crush between palms). Stir gently. Serve hot with naan or paratha.

name: Bhindi Masala
description: Crispy okra tossed with spices, tomatoes, and a touch of amchur for tanginess
steps: Wash okra, dry completely. Trim ends and cut into 1-inch pieces.
Heat oil in a pan on high heat. Add okra and fry without stirring for 3-4 minutes until slightly crispy.
Add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Toss. Add tomato and all spices. Mix gently.
Cook on medium heat for 8-10 minutes until okra is tender and spices coat well. Add amchur.
Garnish with coriander. Serve with roti or dal-rice.

name: Lauki Kofta Curry
description: Fried bottle gourd dumplings in a rich tomato-cashew gravy — a grand Jain main course
steps: Squeeze water from grated lauki. Mix with besan, cornflour, green chilies, and salt. Shape into small balls.
Deep fry koftas on medium heat until golden. Set aside on paper towels.
Blend tomatoes and soaked cashews into a smooth paste.
Heat oil, add cumin and asafoetida. Add tomato-cashew paste and cook for 10 minutes. Add spices, salt, and water to adjust consistency.
Add cream and garam masala. Add koftas just before serving to keep them from getting soggy. Serve with naan.

name: Gujarati Dal
description: Sweet, sour, and spicy toor dal — a signature Gujarati preparation with jaggery and lemon
steps: Pressure cook toor dal with turmeric and 3 cups water for 3 whistles. Mash well.
Add tomato, jaggery, lemon juice, red chili powder, and salt. Simmer for 10 minutes. Adjust sweet-sour balance.
For tadka: heat ghee, add mustard seeds, cumin, cinnamon, cloves, dried red chilies, curry leaves, and asafoetida.
Pour tadka into the dal. Mix well and simmer for 2 minutes.
Garnish with coriander. Serve with steamed rice and a side of papad.

name: Tinda Masala
description: Baby round gourd cooked in a spiced tomato gravy — a light and nutritious everyday sabzi
steps: Peel tinda and cut into quarters. Scoop out seeds if too large.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Add tomatoes and cook until soft.
Add all spice powders and salt. Cook for 2 minutes.
Add tinda pieces and 1/4 cup water. Cover and cook for 12-15 minutes until tender.
Sprinkle amchur and garnish with coriander. Serve with roti.

name: Paneer Bhurji
description: Scrambled paneer with tomatoes, peas, and warm spices — a quick protein-rich dry dish
steps: Heat oil in a pan. Add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Let cumin splutter.
Add green chilies and capsicum. Sauté for 2 minutes.
Add tomatoes and cook until soft. Add peas, turmeric, red chili powder, and salt.
Add crumbled paneer and garam masala. Mix well and cook for 3-4 minutes on medium heat.
Garnish with coriander. Serve with roti, paratha, or as a filling in wraps.

name: Matar Paneer
description: Soft paneer cubes and green peas in a rich spiced tomato gravy — a North Indian classic without onion-garlic
steps: Blend cashews into a smooth paste. Puree tomatoes.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Add tomato puree and cook 10 minutes until oil separates.
Add cashew paste, turmeric, red chili, coriander powder, and salt. Cook 5 minutes.
Add peas and 1/2 cup water. Simmer 8 minutes. Add paneer and garam masala. Cook 3 minutes.
Garnish with coriander. Serve with naan or jeera rice.

name: Baingan Bharta
description: Smoky roasted eggplant mash cooked with tomatoes, peas, and aromatic spices
steps: Roast whole eggplant directly on gas flame, turning frequently until skin is charred and flesh is soft. Cool, peel, and mash.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Add green chilies and tomatoes. Cook until soft.
Add peas, turmeric, red chili, and coriander powder. Cook 3 minutes.
Add mashed eggplant and salt. Mix well and cook on medium heat for 8-10 minutes.
Garnish with coriander. Serve hot with roti.

name: Jain Chole
description: Spiced chickpea curry with a rich tomato-based gravy, tea-infused for dark color — street-food flavor, Jain-style
steps: Soak chickpeas overnight. Pressure cook with tea bag and salt for 5-6 whistles until soft. Discard tea bag.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Add tomato puree and cook 10 minutes.
Add all spice powders and salt. Cook for 5 minutes until thick.
Add cooked chickpeas with some cooking liquid. Simmer 15 minutes. Mash a few chickpeas for thick gravy.
Add amchur and garam masala. Garnish with coriander. Serve with bhatura or puri.

name: Turai Ki Sabzi
description: Simple ridge gourd stir-fry with cumin tempering — a light everyday vegetable side dish
steps: Peel ridge gourd and cut into thin rounds.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Add green chilies.
Add turai, turmeric, red chili powder, and salt. Mix well.
Cover and cook on low heat for 10-12 minutes until tender, stirring occasionally.
Garnish with coriander. Serve with dal and roti.

name: Moong Dal Halwa
description: Rich, decadent halwa made from slow-cooked moong dal with ghee, sugar, and cardamom — a festive favourite
steps: Soak moong dal for 4 hours. Drain and grind to a coarse paste with minimal water.
Heat ghee in a heavy-bottom pan. Add the dal paste and roast on low heat for 25-30 minutes, stirring continuously until golden and aromatic.
Warm milk with saffron. Add to the roasted dal along with sugar. Mix well.
Cook on low heat, stirring frequently, for 10-15 minutes until halwa thickens and ghee separates.
Add cardamom powder, garnish with almonds, cashews, and raisins. Serve warm.

name: Shrikhand
description: Creamy strained yogurt dessert flavored with saffron, cardamom, and pistachios — a Gujarati classic
steps: Tie curd in a muslin cloth and hang for 6-8 hours (or overnight) in the fridge to drain all whey. The hung curd should be thick like cream cheese.
Soak saffron in warm milk for 10 minutes.
Whisk the hung curd until smooth. Add powdered sugar, saffron milk, and cardamom. Mix well.
Chill for at least 2 hours.
Serve cold, garnished with pistachios and almonds.

name: Besan Ladoo
description: Melt-in-mouth gram flour balls sweetened with powdered sugar, ghee, and cardamom
steps: Heat ghee in a heavy-bottom pan. Add besan and roast on low heat for 15-20 minutes, stirring continuously.
The besan should turn deep golden and release a nutty aroma. Be careful not to burn it.
Remove from heat and let cool for 10 minutes (still warm but touchable).
Add powdered sugar, cardamom, almonds, and cashews. Mix well.
While still warm, shape into round balls. Let cool completely. Store in airtight container.

name: Basundi
description: Slow-reduced sweetened milk with saffron, cardamom, and dry fruits — a Maharashtrian celebration dessert
steps: Boil milk in a heavy-bottom pan. Reduce heat and simmer, stirring frequently.
Keep scraping the cream (malai) from the sides and mixing back in.
Reduce to about half — this takes 40-45 minutes. The milk will thicken and turn slightly yellowish.
Add sugar, saffron, and cardamom. Cook for 5 more minutes.
Let cool, then chill. Serve cold garnished with almonds, pistachios, and charoli.

name: Rice Kheer
description: Classic Indian rice pudding simmered in milk with cardamom, saffron, and dry fruits
steps: Wash and soak rice for 30 minutes. Drain.
Boil milk in a heavy-bottom pan. Add drained rice. Cook on low heat, stirring frequently, for 30 minutes.
When rice is completely soft and milk has thickened, add sugar and saffron. Cook 5 more minutes.
Fry raisins in ghee until plump. Add to kheer with almonds and cardamom.
Serve warm or chilled.

name: Suji Ka Halwa
description: Golden semolina pudding cooked in ghee with sugar, saffron, and dry fruits — ready in 15 minutes
steps: Heat ghee in a pan. Fry cashews and raisins until golden. Remove and set aside.
Add semolina to the same ghee. Roast on low heat for 8-10 minutes until golden and fragrant.
Dissolve sugar in warm water with saffron. Slowly pour into the roasted semolina (careful — it will splutter). Stir vigorously.
Cook on low heat for 3-4 minutes until all water is absorbed and halwa comes together.
Add cardamom and fried dry fruits. Serve warm.

name: Coconut Barfi
description: Fudgy coconut sweets made with condensed milk, cardamom, and a hint of saffron — sets in minutes
steps: Grease a plate or tray with ghee.
In a non-stick pan, mix coconut and condensed milk. Cook on low heat, stirring continuously for 8-10 minutes.
When the mixture thickens and starts leaving the sides, add cardamom and saffron. Mix well.
Transfer to the greased plate. Spread evenly. Press pistachios on top.
Let cool and set for 1-2 hours. Cut into diamond shapes. Store in an airtight container.

name: Jeera Rice
description: Fragrant basmati rice with cumin seeds and ghee — the perfect everyday accompaniment
steps: Wash rice and soak for 30 minutes. Drain.
Heat ghee, add cumin seeds, bay leaf, cloves, and cardamom. Sauté until cumin splutters.
Add drained rice and gently stir for 1 minute.
Add water and salt. Bring to a boil, then reduce to lowest heat. Cover tightly and cook for 15 minutes.
Do not open the lid. Turn off heat and let rest for 5 minutes. Fluff with a fork and serve.

name: Vegetable Pulao
description: Fragrant one-pot rice with mixed vegetables, whole spices, and ghee — festive yet simple
steps: Wash and soak rice for 30 minutes. Drain.
Heat ghee, add whole spices (cumin, bay leaf, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom) and asafoetida.
Add vegetables and sauté for 2 minutes. Add turmeric and salt.
Add rice, mix gently. Add water and mint. Bring to boil, reduce heat, cover and cook 15 minutes.
Rest for 5 minutes. Fluff with fork. Serve with raita.

name: Plain Roti
description: Soft whole wheat flatbreads puffed over direct flame — the foundation of every Indian meal
steps: Mix flour and salt. Add water gradually and knead into a soft, smooth dough. Rest for 10 minutes.
Divide into golf-ball-sized portions. Roll each into a thin circle using dry flour for dusting.
Heat a tawa on medium-high. Place roti and cook until small bubbles appear. Flip.
Using tongs, place directly on flame to puff up. Flip once.
Apply ghee if desired. Serve immediately with any dal or sabzi.

name: Puri
description: Golden deep-fried puffed bread — crispy outside, soft inside, perfect with chole or halwa
steps: Mix flour, semolina, salt, and oil. Knead into a stiff dough with water. Rest 10 minutes.
Divide into small balls. Roll each into a small circle (not too thin).
Heat oil to 180°C. Gently slide a puri into the hot oil.
It should puff up within seconds. Flip and fry until golden on both sides. Drain.
Serve immediately with chole, halwa, or any gravy dish.

name: Masala Chaas
description: Spiced buttermilk with roasted cumin, black salt, and fresh mint — the ultimate Indian digestive cooler
steps: Whisk curd until smooth. Add chilled water and whisk again until frothy.
Add roasted cumin powder, black salt, and regular salt.
Finely chop mint and coriander. Add to the buttermilk.
Add minced green chili if you like it spicy.
Serve chilled in tall glasses with ice cubes.

name: Mango Lassi
description: Thick, creamy yogurt smoothie blended with sweet mango pulp and a hint of cardamom
steps: Blend curd, mango pulp, sugar, milk, and cardamom until smooth and frothy.
Add a few ice cubes and blend briefly.
Pour into tall glasses. Garnish with saffron strands.
Serve immediately.

name: Thandai
description: Rich milk drink infused with almonds, fennel, cardamom, poppy seeds, and rose water — a Holi special
steps: Soak almonds, cashews, poppy seeds, fennel seeds, and melon seeds in water for 4 hours.
Drain and grind into a fine paste with 1/4 cup milk. Add cardamom and peppercorns while grinding.
Warm remaining milk with sugar until dissolved. Let cool.
Strain the nut-spice paste through a muslin cloth into the milk, pressing to extract all flavor.
Add saffron and rose water. Chill for 2 hours. Serve cold garnished with slivered almonds.

name: Kokum Sherbet
description: Tangy, sweet, and cooling kokum drink with roasted cumin and black salt — a Konkan coast classic
steps: Soak kokum in 1 cup warm water for 1 hour. Squeeze and extract all the color and flavor. Strain.
Mix kokum extract with remaining water, sugar, cumin powder, black salt, and regular salt.
Stir until sugar dissolves completely.
Chill for at least 1 hour. Serve cold with mint leaves and ice.

name: Gobi Matar
description: Cauliflower florets and green peas in a dry spiced preparation — an everyday comfort sabzi
steps: Heat oil, add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Let cumin splutter.
Add cauliflower florets. Toss on high heat for 2 minutes for slight char.
Add turmeric, red chili, coriander powder, and salt. Mix well.
Add peas and 2 tbsp water. Cover and cook on low heat for 12-15 minutes until cauliflower is tender but not mushy.
Add garam masala, garnish with coriander. Serve with roti.

name: Jain Dal Makhani
description: Creamy black lentils slow-cooked with butter, cream, and spices — restaurant richness without onion or garlic
steps: Soak urad dal and rajma overnight. Pressure cook with salt and enough water for 6-7 whistles until very soft.
In a separate pan, melt butter. Add asafoetida and tomato puree. Cook 10 minutes until thick.
Add all spice powders and cook 2 minutes. Add this to the cooked dal.
Simmer on very low heat for 30 minutes, stirring occasionally. The longer it cooks, the creamier it gets.
Add cream and kasuri methi. Mix well. Serve with a dollop of butter on top with naan.

name: Kadhi Pakoda
description: Tangy yogurt-besan curry with crispy besan fritters — a classic North Indian comfort meal
steps: For pakodas: mix 1/2 cup besan with capsicum, pinch of salt, and water to make thick batter. Drop small spoonfuls into hot oil, fry until golden. Set aside.
For kadhi: whisk curd, 4 tbsp besan, water, turmeric, red chili powder, and salt until smooth and lump-free.
Bring the curd mixture to a boil, stirring continuously. Reduce heat and simmer 15 minutes.
For tadka: heat ghee, add cumin, mustard seeds, dried chilies, curry leaves, and asafoetida. Pour into the kadhi.
Add pakodas to the kadhi. Simmer 5 minutes. Serve hot with steamed rice.

name: Undhiyu
description: A grand Gujarati mixed vegetable casserole with muthia dumplings, cooked upside down in spiced oil
steps: For muthia: mix besan, coriander, coconut, sesame seeds, green chilies, spices, oil, and salt into a stiff dough. Shape into small cylinders. Steam for 15 minutes, then slice.
Slit small brinjals into quarters without separating. Stuff with a paste of coconut, coriander, sesame, and spices.
In a heavy pot, heat oil. Add asafoetida. Layer vegetables: beans, peas, cauliflower, stuffed brinjals, and muthia on top.
Sprinkle remaining spices, sugar, and 1/4 cup water. Cover tightly and cook on low heat for 25-30 minutes.
Gently mix once before serving. Serve with puri.

name: Cabbage Peas Stir Fry
description: Quick dry sabzi of shredded cabbage and peas with mustard seeds and curry leaves
steps: Heat oil. Add mustard seeds, cumin seeds, curry leaves, green chilies, and asafoetida.
Add shredded cabbage, peas, turmeric, and salt. Toss on high heat for 2 minutes.
Reduce heat, cover and cook for 8-10 minutes until cabbage is tender but still has a bite.
Garnish with coconut and coriander. Serve with dal and rice.

name: Corn Palak
description: Sweet corn kernels in a vibrant spinach gravy — iron-rich, protein-rich, and delicious
steps: Blanch spinach in boiling water for 2 minutes. Transfer to ice water. Blend with green chilies into a smooth puree.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Add tomato and cook until soft.
Add spinach puree, turmeric, and salt. Cook 5 minutes.
Add corn kernels and simmer for 8 minutes. Add garam masala and cream.
Serve hot with roti or naan.

name: Mixed Dal Fry
description: A protein-packed blend of three lentils tempered with cumin, tomato, and ghee
steps: Wash all dals together. Pressure cook with turmeric, salt, and 3.5 cups water for 3-4 whistles. Mash well.
Add tomatoes, red chili, and coriander powder to the dal. Simmer 10 minutes.
For tadka: heat ghee, add cumin seeds, dried red chilies, and asafoetida.
Pour tadka over dal. Mix well and simmer 2 minutes.
Garnish with coriander. Serve with rice or roti.

name: Jain Paneer Capsicum Masala
description: Chunky paneer and colorful capsicum in a thick spiced tomato gravy — a main-course showstopper
steps: Grind soaked cashews with a little water to a smooth paste.
Heat oil, add cumin seeds and asafoetida. Add tomato puree and cook 8 minutes.
Add cashew paste, turmeric, chili powder, coriander powder, and salt. Cook 5 minutes.
Add capsicum chunks and paneer. Add 1/4 cup water. Simmer 5 minutes.
Finish with garam masala and kasuri methi. Garnish with coriander. Serve with naan.

name: Sprouts Chaat
description: Tangy, crunchy sprouted moong salad with tomatoes, capsicum, lemon, and chaat masala
steps: Steam sprouts for 5 minutes until just cooked but still crunchy. Cool.
Mix with tomato, capsicum, cucumber, and green chilies.
Add lemon juice, chaat masala, cumin powder, black salt, and regular salt. Toss well.
Garnish with coriander and sev. Serve immediately.

name: Jain Pani Puri
description: Crispy hollow puris filled with sprouted moong and tangy mint water — street food made Jain-safe
steps: For pani: blend mint, coriander, green chilies with 1 cup water. Strain. Add remaining water, lemon juice, cumin powder, black salt, and salt. Chill.
Steam sprouts and season with chaat masala and a pinch of salt.
Crack a hole in each puri. Fill with a spoonful of sprouts.
Add a drizzle of tamarind chutney and fill with the chilled mint pani.
Serve immediately — eat in one bite!

name: Jain Dabeli
description: Gujarati spiced pav filled with sweet-spicy mashed peas and pomegranate — the street snack reinvented without potato
steps: Mix mashed peas with dabeli masala, 1 tbsp tamarind chutney, and salt. Heat in a pan for 3-4 minutes.
Slit pavs horizontally. Apply green chutney on one side and tamarind chutney on the other.
Fill with the peas mixture. Top with pomegranate seeds and crushed peanuts.
Toast on a buttered tawa until golden on both sides.
Serve with sev sprinkled on top and extra chutneys on the side.

name: Jain Dahi Vada
description: Soft urad dal dumplings soaked in seasoned yogurt with tamarind chutney and spices
steps: Soak urad dal for 4 hours. Grind to a fluffy batter with green chilies and minimal water. Add salt and cumin seeds.
Heat oil. Drop spoonfuls of batter and fry on medium heat until golden. Soak fried vadas in warm water for 20 minutes.
Whisk curd with sugar, salt, and roasted cumin powder until smooth.
Squeeze water from vadas gently. Place in a dish, pour whisked curd over them.
Top with tamarind chutney, red chili powder, cumin powder, and coriander. Chill and serve cold.

name: Masala Puri Chaat
description: Crushed puris topped with sprouted moong, chutneys, sev, and crunchy bits — a Bangalore street classic
steps: Lightly crush puris on a plate — they should be broken but not powdered.
Top with steamed sprouts, tomato, and capsicum.
Drizzle green chutney and tamarind chutney generously.
Sprinkle chaat masala and lemon juice. Top with sev and coriander.
Serve immediately while puris are still crunchy.

name: Sabudana Vada
description: Crispy tapioca pearl fritters with peanuts and cumin — a popular fasting snack that's addictive any day
steps: Soak sabudana for 6 hours in just enough water. Drain completely — it should be fluffy and separate.
Mix with ground peanuts, green chilies, coriander, cumin, lemon juice, and salt.
Shape into flat patties. If mixture is loose, add a little rice flour or arrowroot powder.
Deep fry on medium heat until golden and crispy on both sides.
Serve hot with green chutney and curd.

name: Dal Baati Churma
description: Hard wheat balls baked and dunked in ghee, served with panchmel dal and sweet churma — Rajasthan's crown jewel
steps: For baati: mix wheat flour, semolina, ghee, salt, and water into a stiff dough. Shape into round balls. Bake at 180°C for 30 minutes until golden and hard.
For dal: wash all dals, pressure cook with turmeric and salt for 4 whistles. Mash. Temper with cumin, asafoetida, tomato, and spices.
For churma: crumble 2 baatis, mix with ghee, sugar, and cardamom powder.
Break open hot baatis, dunk in ghee, pour dal over them.
Serve with churma on the side — the sweet and savory combination is divine.

name: Jain Pav Bhaji
description: Mumbai street-style spiced vegetable mash with buttered pav — made with cauliflower, peas, and capsicum instead of potato
steps: Boil cauliflower, peas, beans, and capsicum until soft. Drain and mash roughly.
Heat 2 tbsp butter. Add tomatoes and cook until mushy. Add pav bhaji masala, turmeric, chili powder, and salt.
Add mashed vegetables. Mix well and cook on medium heat for 10 minutes, mashing continuously.
Add 1/2 cup water if too thick. Add lemon juice and remaining butter.
Toast pavs on butter. Serve bhaji garnished with coriander, a pat of butter, and chopped capsicum.

name: Paneer Pulao
description: Aromatic basmati rice cooked with spiced paneer cubes, peas, and whole spices
steps: Soak rice for 30 minutes. Drain.
Heat ghee, lightly fry paneer cubes until golden. Remove and set aside.
In the same ghee, add whole spices and asafoetida. Add peas and rice. Stir gently for 1 minute.
Add water, turmeric, salt, and mint. Bring to boil, reduce heat, cover. Cook 15 minutes.
Gently fold in fried paneer. Rest 5 minutes. Fluff and serve with raita.

name: Missi Roti
description: Spiced flatbread made from a mix of wheat flour and gram flour — packed with flavor and protein
steps: Mix both flours, all spices, coriander, green chilies, and oil. Knead into a firm dough with water. Rest 10 minutes.
Divide into portions. Roll each into a thick circle (thicker than regular roti).
Cook on a hot tawa. When bubbles appear, flip. Apply ghee and press with a cloth.
Cook both sides until golden brown spots appear.
Serve hot with curd, pickle, or any dal.

name: Lemon Rice
description: Tangy South Indian rice with lemon juice, peanuts, and a mustard-curry leaf tempering
steps: Heat oil. Add mustard seeds, urad dal, chana dal, and peanuts. Fry until golden.
Add curry leaves, green chilies, turmeric, and asafoetida. Sauté 30 seconds.
Add cooled rice and salt. Mix gently without breaking the grains.
Turn off heat. Add lemon juice and mix well.
Serve with papad and pickle.

name: Jaljeera
description: Zesty cumin-mint cooler with tangy amchur and black salt — the ultimate summer thirst quencher
steps: Blend mint, coriander, and 1/2 cup water into a smooth paste. Strain through a sieve.
Mix the strained liquid with remaining water, cumin, black salt, amchur, pepper, lemon juice, sugar, and salt.
Stir well until everything is dissolved.
Serve in tall glasses with ice cubes and a sprig of mint.

name: Sweet Lassi
description: Thick creamy yogurt drink blended with sugar and cardamom — Punjab's gift to the world
steps: Blend curd, sugar, water, and cardamom until smooth and frothy.
Pour into tall glasses. Top with saffron strands and crushed pistachios.
Serve chilled.

name: Lauki Ka Halwa
description: A delightful bottle gourd halwa that rivals gajar halwa — slow-cooked in milk with cardamom and dry fruits
steps: Heat ghee in a heavy-bottom pan. Add grated lauki and cook on medium heat for 10 minutes until water evaporates.
Add milk and saffron. Cook on low heat, stirring occasionally, for 25-30 minutes until milk is absorbed.
Add sugar and mix. Cook for another 10 minutes until halwa thickens and ghee separates.
Add cardamom powder. Fry dry fruits in a little ghee and add to halwa.
Serve warm or chilled.

name: Gatte Ki Sabzi
description: Steamed gram flour dumplings in a tangy yogurt-based curry — a Rajasthani signature dish
steps: Mix besan, 1 tbsp oil, ajwain, turmeric, red chili powder, and salt. Knead into a firm dough with water. Shape into long cylinders.
Boil water. Cook gatte in boiling water for 15 minutes. Remove and cut into 1-inch pieces. Save the water.
Whisk curd with 2 tbsp besan and 1 cup gatte water until smooth.
Heat oil, add cumin and asafoetida. Add curd mixture and all spices. Cook on low heat, stirring, for 10 minutes.
Add gatte pieces and garam masala. Simmer 5 minutes. Garnish with coriander. Serve with roti or rice.

name: Rasmalai
description: Soft paneer discs soaked in cardamom-saffron reduced milk — the queen of Indian desserts
steps: Boil 1L milk, add lemon juice to curdle. Strain through muslin cloth. Wash under cold water. Squeeze out water and knead chenna for 5 minutes until smooth.
Shape into small flat discs. Make sugar syrup (1 cup sugar + 4 cups water). Boil discs in syrup for 10 minutes — they will expand. Let cool in syrup.
For rabri: boil 750ml milk, reduce on low heat for 25 minutes, scraping malai and mixing. Add sugar, cardamom, saffron.
Squeeze sugar syrup from the discs. Add them to the rabri. Refrigerate 4 hours.
Serve chilled, garnished with pistachios, almonds, and rose water.

name: Vermicelli Upma
description: Roasted thin vermicelli tossed with peas, peanuts, and a mustard-curry leaf tempering
steps: Dry roast vermicelli in a pan until light golden. Set aside.
Heat oil. Add mustard seeds, urad dal, chana dal, and peanuts. Fry until golden.
Add curry leaves, green chilies, and asafoetida. Add peas and turmeric.
Add water and salt. Bring to boil. Add roasted vermicelli. Stir, cover, cook on low for 3-4 minutes.
Fluff with fork, add lemon juice, and garnish with coriander.

name: Green Coriander Chutney
description: Vibrant fresh coriander-mint chutney — the essential condiment for every Indian meal
steps: Wash coriander and mint leaves thoroughly.
Blend all ingredients together into a smooth paste, using minimal water.
Adjust salt and lemon juice to taste.
Store in a glass jar in the fridge — stays fresh for 3-4 days.
